slides.title: Build Lists in One Line
slides.body: A **list comprehension** turns a multi-line `for` loop into a single, readable expression. It's one of Python's most loved shortcuts.
slides.tip: Same result as a loop — just faster to write and often easier to read.

slides.title: Loop vs. Comprehension
slides.body: These two snippets do **exactly the same thing**. The comprehension reads almost like English: *"give me `n * 2` for each `n` in `numbers`"*.
slides.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
doubled = [n * 2 for n in numbers]
print(doubled)

slides.title: Transform Anything
slides.body: The expression before `for` can be **any valid Python** — call a method, apply math, or format a string.
slides.code:
words = ["hello", "world", "python"]
upper = [w.upper() for w in words]
print(upper)

slides.title: Filter with if
slides.body: Add `if condition` at the **end** to skip items that don't qualify. Only items where the condition is `True` make it into the new list.
slides.code:
numbers = [-3, -1, 0, 2, 4, 7]
positives = [x for x in numbers if x > 0]
print(positives)
slides.tip: You can combine a transform AND a filter: [x * 2 for x in numbers if x > 0]

slides.title: You've Got This!
slides.body: List comprehensions follow one simple pattern: `[expression for item in iterable]`. Add `if` to filter, swap brackets for `{}` to get a dict or set comprehension.
slides.tip: When a comprehension feels hard to read, a plain for loop is always a valid choice.
